The most important thing to this type of negotiator is the relationship. As long as the relationship is being worked on, accommodators view time as being well spent. Their main goal is to be on great terms with their counterpart and they often get caught up with the idea of win-win. Unfortunately, being fair to you is so important to the relationship and their mind, they tend to put your needs before their own. All the three types, they are most likely to build great rapport without actually accomplishing anything. But for them, a solid foundational relationship is 95% of the path to agreement. Even if an agreement can't be reached, they want to remain friends. They will yield the concession to appease or concede in hopes that the other side will reciprocate. That's how they end up with one-sided deals that may never get implemented. Some basic traits of accommodators are sociable, peace-seeking, optimistic, distractable, and poor time managers. You ever have someone call you and when you see their name on your phone, you already know it's a 45-minute conversation? That may be a huge indicator of accommodators in your life. Refrain from getting frustrated by their approach to negotiation because their focus is on getting to know the person behind the table. They have a tremendous passion for the spirit of negotiation and feel that spirit must be protected. Although it is very easy to disagree with an accommodator, uncovering their objections can be extremely difficult because they want to hear what you have to say. They're also afraid that their objections will be detrimental to the relationship. Their view of the white lie, which is not an entirely honest response, is much, much broader than the other two types. As possible, they will agree to something they can't actually deliver in order to protect their relationship. They will not intentionally deceive you, but will do whatever it takes to build, maintain, and protect how you see them and their relationship. Because of this, it's important to use calibrated questions focused on implementation. This will nudge them along and encourage them gently to find ways to translate their talk into action. Before the negotiation, they will have likely identified potential problem areas and may seek to leave those areas unaddressed, out of fear of the conflict they might cause. Addressing problem areas with labels is a great way to ease them into resolving conflict. Accommodators are horrified of silence because that is what they use to signal fury. If you need to go silent in the process of what's going on, let them know or they will become anxious their relationship is in peril and consequently fill that silence with talk. If they've been offended, an apology is essential. Not receiving one will cause a distraction to them which will interfere with their ability to collaborate effectively with you, ever. If this description sounds like you, number one, please use your ability to build relationships, but do not sacrifice your objections. The other two types need to hear your point of view. Number two, you can still be who you are and stand your ground firmly. Assertion doesn't require aggression nor bluntness. Also, be conscious of your excess chit chat. Unfortunately, the other two styles have little use for it. If you're meeting with someone like yourself, you may be prone to interactions where nothing gets accomplished. Number three, you have less to learn about negotiation than you think. You're already built to create rapport. The great strength of the accommodator is their naturally likable demeanor and friendly tone. You're six times more likely to make a deal with someone you like and this is a huge strategic advantage. Click on one of the other options to learn more about the other negotiation types.
